identity: Trehalose = alpha-D-glucopyranosyl-(1->1)-alpha-D-glucopyranoside, a NON-REDUCING disaccharide of two alpha-glucose units linked 1,1-glycosidically. Anhydrous: C12H22O11, MW 342.30 g/mol, CAS 99-20-7. Dihydrate (the normal commercial crystalline form): C12H22O11.2H2O, MW 378.33 g/mol, CAS 6138-23-4. Synonyms: D-(+)-trehalose, alpha,alpha-trehalose, mycose, mushroom sugar. Odorless white crystals, ~45% sweetness of sucrose. (Tier 2: Wikipedia/Trehalose; Tier 3 confirm: ChemicalBook CB4454890, BOC Sciences 304806.)
r1_solubility: Aqueous solubility ~68.9 g/100 g water at 20 C, i.e. ~689 mg/mL (Tier 2: Wikipedia/Trehalose). Less soluble than sucrose below ~80 C but freely water-soluble at the concentrations any ORS/syrup would use. INTENDED USE in this formula: as a stabilizer/bulking sugar it would only ever be needed at low single-digit % (e.g. 1-5 g/L in a drink, or simply as a dry co-solute / lyo-bulking agent in the dry SKUs). Headroom vs an intended ~5-50 mg/mL in-solution use = ~14x to ~138x. >>3 => OK (no solubility constraint). Note the dihydrate is the crystalline grade; it carries ~9.5% water of crystallization, which is irrelevant once dissolved but matters for dry-blend moisture (see Cautions). OK.
r2_stability: Trehalose is among the MOST stable sugars in solution. (1) NON-REDUCING: the two anomeric carbons are joined in the 1,1 linkage, so it has no free reducing group and effectively does NOT participate in Maillard browning with amines/amino acids/B6 — in model systems brown-color rate constants were ~200-2000x LOWER for trehalose than sucrose at pH 2.5 (Tier 2: Patil/Stability of Trehalose, Sucrose and Glucose to Nonenzymatic Browning, ResearchGate pub 229729124). (2) ACID/THERMAL: the 1,1-glycosidic bond is low-energy (~1 kcal/mol vs ~27 for sucrose); activation energy for acid hydrolysis is ~2x that of other disaccharides, so trehalose resists acid hydrolysis and is stable in hot/acidic solution (Tier 2: biomedres.us BJSTR.MS.ID.003608; Wikipedia/Trehalose). This means at the formula dry-acidified target (drink pH ~3.8-4.2) and at the wet-formula pH band (~5.8-6.2) trehalose does NOT hydrolyze to glucose appreciably and does NOT brown. No photo/oxidative lability. (3) High glass-transition Tg => excellent DRY-state lyo/cryo-protectant and glassy-matrix former, protecting co-dried labile actives (B12, P5P, NAC thiol) by water-replacement/vitrification (Tier 1: PMC11510631 Lyoprotectants in Protein Stabilization; PMC11129304 / J Phys Chem B 10.1021/acs.jpcb.4c00022 Sucrose vs Trehalose by DSC). Stable pH window: broad, comfortably spans both 3.8-4.2 and 5.8-6.2. Shelf-life: effectively indefinite in solution chemically; the ONLY shelf risk is microbial (it is a fermentable sugar) and water uptake of the dihydrate — both solved by the dry/make-fresh design. Microbial preservation is NOT improved or worsened by trehalose vs other sugars; same make-fresh discipline applies.
r3_conflicts: Pairwise vs current/redesigned ingredients: (a) Reducing sugars + amino acids/B6 (the formula's worst Maillard stack: S2 corn syrup+maltodextrin+ribose+Na-ascorbate; citrulline/taurine/betaine amines): trehalose is non-reducing and would REPLACE a reducing sugar -> mechanism: removes a Maillard reactant -> mitigation IS the substitution; net stability benefit (Tier 2: browning model systems, pub 229729124). (b) Dextrose/glucose in the fight-day ORS: no conflict, but note trehalose is poorly absorbed intact via SGLT1 and must be cleaved by intestinal trehalase to 2 glucose first; it does NOT substitute for dextrose as the SGLT1 cotransport driver (the drink's glucose:Na ratio must stay built on free glucose) -> mechanism: trehalase-dependent uptake, slower osmotically -> mitigation: keep dextrose as the SGLT1 sugar; use trehalose only as a stabilizer/bulking sugar, not as the rehydration carbohydrate. (c) Divalent metals (Mg/Ca/Zn) + citrate/phosphate: trehalose is non-ionic, non-chelating -> NO precipitation/redox interaction; it can co-exist with electrolytes without binding. (d) Ascorbate/NAC-thiol oxidation (Fe/Cu catalysis): trehalose is redox-inert, neither accelerates nor protects against metal-catalyzed oxidation in solution (its protection is a DRY glassy-matrix effect, not an aqueous antioxidant effect) -> in the dry premix it helps preserve B12/P5P/NAC by vitrification; in a wet product it gives no antioxidant benefit. (e) Osmolality: each g/L adds ~2.6 mOsm/kg (MW 342) — at 5 g/L only ~15 mOsm/kg, negligible vs the <300 cap; do NOT load it high in the hypotonic ORS or it eats the hypotonic headroom.
avian_evidence: DIRECT AVIAN DATA EXISTS (Tier 1). Yu et al. 2025, "Trehalose improves growth performance and gut health of yellow-feathered broilers exposed to heat stress," Tropical Animal Health and Production 57:414, DOI 10.1007/s11250-025-04665-4. Design: 240 day-old broilers, 4 groups — thermoneutral control, heat-stress + basal (HS0, 33+/-2 C), HS + 0.1% dietary trehalose, HS + 0.5% dietary trehalose. Findings: 0.5% trehalose raised 28-d body weight vs HS0/HS0.1; reversed the heat-stress-induced drop in serum total antioxidant capacity and rise in malondialdehyde (lipid peroxidation marker); mitigated the heat-stress rise in ALT at 56 d; modulated gut microbiota. So as a DIETARY (dry feed) additive at ~0.5% of feed (~5 g/kg feed; ~0.6-0.75 g/bird/day at 120-150 g intake) trehalose is avian-evidenced for heat-stress antioxidant/gut-health support and growth — a CHRONIC, feed-delivered mechanism, NOT an acute drink lever. No avian data found for trehalose as an in-water ORS sugar or for acute single-session benefit; that use would be as a formulation stabilizer only, not an evidenced active. Poultry intestinal trehalase activity is the rate-limit on systemic glucose yield from trehalose (general mammalian/avian brush-border enzyme; UNKNOWN quantitative chicken trehalase Vmax — see blocking gaps).
verdict: ADD-conditional, in TWO narrow roles, mostly the DRY PRELOAD: (1) PRELOAD (dry feed premix): include trehalose at ~0.5% of feed (~5 g/kg feed, ~0.6-0.75 g/bird/day) as an avian-evidenced chronic heat-stress antioxidant/gut-health adjunct AND as a dry glassy-matrix stabilizer/carrier that protects co-blended labile actives (B12, P5P, NAC) by vitrification — this is where the real evidence and the real stabilizer value land (Yu 2025; PMC11510631). (2) FORMULATION STABILIZER, optional: if any sugar bulking/anti-browning is wanted in a dry stick, trehalose is the correct non-reducing choice over corn syrup/maltodextrin/ribose. SKIP it as the fight-day ORS rehydration carbohydrate — keep DEXTROSE for SGLT1; trehalose needs trehalase cleavage and is not the cotransport sugar. Do NOT add to wet liquids for "stability" — its protection is a dry-state effect, and the redesign deletes the liquids anyway. Net: a genuinely useful, very-stable, non-Maillard sugar whose best home is the dry preload, secondarily a dry-stick bulking aid.
cautions: Notes (non-blocking): (1) Trehalose is a fermentable sugar — in any reconstituted wet drink it supports microbial growth identically to other sugars; rely on the make-fresh-daily / acidified / discard-after-session discipline, not on trehalose for preservation. (2) Dihydrate grade carries ~9.5% crystal water and is mildly hygroscopic in bulk; in a dry premix store desiccated/foil so it does not cake or donate moisture to NAC/B12. (3) It is NOT a free-glucose source on its own — uptake requires intestinal trehalase; do not count its sugar toward SGLT1 rehydration math or toward immediate energy delivery. (4) The avian benefit is at a CHRONIC ~0.5% feed dose over days; an acute in-drink sip has no evidenced benefit, so its drink use is stabilizer-only. (5) Welfare-positive framing holds: the evidenced effect is the bird's OWN antioxidant capacity, lower lipid peroxidation, lower ALT, and better gut health under heat stress. (6) A human-medicine signal exists that high oral trehalose can expand toxigenic C. difficile lineages in mammals — UNKNOWN relevance to chickens and not at supplement-scale feed doses, but worth a one-line monitoring note on gut-health scale-up.
sources: Yu et al. 2025. Trehalose improves growth performance and gut health of yellow-feathered broilers exposed to heat stress. Tropical Animal Health and Production 57:414. DOI 10.1007/s11250-025-04665-4 — Tier 1 (peer-reviewed, direct avian)
PMC11510631. Effectiveness of Lyoprotectants in Protein Stabilization During Lyophilization. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11510631/ — Tier 1 (review, dry glassy-matrix/lyoprotection)
Comparison of Sucrose and Trehalose for Protein Stabilization Using DSC. J Phys Chem B, DOI 10.1021/acs.jpcb.4c00022 (PMC11129304) — Tier 1 (peer-reviewed, Tg/stabilization)
Stability of Trehalose, Sucrose and Glucose to Nonenzymatic Browning in Model Systems. ResearchGate publication 229729124 — Tier 2 (browning rate constants ~200-2000x lower than sucrose at pH 2.5)
biomedres.us BJSTR.MS.ID.003608 'The Importance of Trehalose Sugar' — Tier 2 (1,1-linkage low energy ~1 vs 27 kcal/mol; ~2x acid-hydrolysis activation energy; non-reducing/Maillard-inert)
Wikipedia: Trehalose (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trehalose) — Tier 2 (solubility 68.9 g/100 g at 20 C; ~45% sucrose sweetness; non-reducing structure)
ChemicalBook CB4454890 (CAS 6138-23-4 dihydrate, MW 378.33) and CAS 99-20-7 anhydrous MW 342.30 — Tier 3 (identity/CAS/MW confirmation only)
blocking_gaps: UNKNOWN: quantitative chicken intestinal trehalase activity (Vmax/expression vs age and vs SGLT1) — needed to confirm trehalose cannot meaningfully substitute for dextrose in the SGLT1 ORS; treat as non-carrier until sourced (no PMID found).

UNKNOWN: exact aqueous solubility figure AT 25 C specifically (search returned 20 C = 689 mg/mL); 25 C value not located in a Tier-1/2 monograph — solubility verdict (OK) is robust to this gap given >14x headroom, but log as a minor citation gap.
UNKNOWN: relevance to chickens of the mammalian high-dose-trehalose / C. difficile-lineage signal — flag for gut-health monitoring on scale-up; no avian data found.
UNKNOWN: no avian data for trehalose as an in-WATER acute sugar; in-drink use is stabilizer-only, not an evidenced active (only the 0.5% dry-feed chronic dose is avian-evidenced).

corrections: TIER UPGRADE (the browning citation is mis-tiered): the '200-2000x lower browning' figure traces to O'Brien J. 1996, 'Stability of Trehalose, Sucrose and Glucose to Nonenzymatic Browning in Model Systems,' Journal of Food Science 61(4):679-682, DOI 10.1111/j.1365-2621.1996.tb12180.x. This is a peer-reviewed Tier-1 primary source, not the Tier-2 'ResearchGate publication 229729124' the dossier cites. Upgrade the citation: cite the JFS DOI as Tier 1 (the ResearchGate number is just an index entry for the same paper). The 200-2000x figure and 'sucrose hydrolysis approaching glucose browning rates' are confirmed verbatim.

R1 temperature caveat is correctly self-flagged but state it inline: the 689 mg/mL solubility is at 20 C (Wikipedia / Illinois Experts 'Water activity and solubility of trehalose'); a Tier-1/2 25 C monograph value was NOT located. Verdict OK is robust given >14x headroom; keep this as the logged minor citation gap (already in blocking_gaps).

PMC11510631 adds a supporting nuance the dossier should fold into Caution #2: that review explicitly notes trehalose DIHYDRATE crystallization can destabilize mAb formulations on frozen storage (sucrose is sometimes added to suppress it). This strengthens the existing 'store desiccated / dihydrate caking' caution rather than contradicting it.

C. difficile signal: pin the citation precisely - Collins et al. 2018, 'Dietary trehalose enhances virulence of epidemic Clostridium difficile,' Nature 553:291-294, DOI 10.1038/nature25178, PMID 29310122 (RT027 point mutation ~500x sensitivity; RT078 four-gene PTS cluster). Dossier's framing as UNKNOWN avian relevance / non-blocking monitoring note is appropriate; just add the real PMID/DOI.
_verify.notes: Adversarial spot-check of all load-bearing sources via WebSearch. Avian anchor (Yu et al. 2025, Trop Anim Health Prod 57:414, DOI 10.1007/s11250-025-04665-4) is REAL and matches the dossier exactly: 240 day-old yellow-feathered broilers, 4 groups (control 24C, HS0 33C, HS0.1, HS0.5), 0.5% trehalose raised 28-d BW vs HS0/HS0.1, reversed HS-induced TAC drop and MDA rise, mitigated 56-d ALT rise, modulated microbiota. No inversion of dose, group structure, or direction of effect. Physicochem confirmed: non-reducing 1,1-alpha-glucopyranoside, solubility 68.9 g/100g@20C, acid-hydrolysis resistance, 200-2000x lower browning than sucrose (O'Brien 1996). Lyoprotection (PMC11510631) and DSC Tg (PMC11129304) real. C. difficile signal real (Nature 2018). The R3 logic is avian-sound and matches the frame: trehalose needs trehalase cleavage so is correctly excluded as the SGLT1/dextrose rehydration sugar; redox-inert (no ascorbate/NAC interaction in solution); non-ionic/non-chelating with Mg/citrate; protection is a dry-state vitrification effect not an aqueous antioxidant effect; osmolality contribution negligible at low g/L. Verdict (ADD-conditional, dry-preload primary + dry-stick stabilizer, NOT a wet-liquid 'stability' additive, NOT the ORS carb) is well-supported and consistent with the redesign deleting liquids. Only corrections are citation tiering/precision, not substance. CONFIRMED. Files reviewed: none on disk - verification was source-level via WebSearch against the provided draft JSON.
